_comment: Top-level overview content. lede = opening paragraph. callouts = 4 boxes. key_patterns = enumerated descriptive findings. observed_patterns = the section formerly called 'best_practices' — descriptive, not prescriptive. age_analysis is its own block. Numbers come from scripts/build.py computations; rerun build after adding pitchers and refresh prose if conclusions shift.
lede: A descriptive study of how MLB organizations manage young, valued starting pitchers during their first full affiliated MiLB season. Surfaces visible in-game starter usage — pitch counts, rest, low-IP outings, build-up shape, response to promotions — derived from game logs only. Currently covers 24 pitchers across 11 organizations, 2024-2025 seasons, ages 18-23, first full pro debuts through AAA. The sample is intentionally focused on arms that threw enough starts for management patterns to be visible; survivorship bias is a feature of the design, not a flaw. Not a measure of total throwing workload (bullpens / catch / live BP are not in the CSVs) and not an injury prediction tool.

callouts.headline: Four pitchers' seasons sat almost entirely inside the 0.8–1.3 ACWR band.
callouts.body: Ford (SEA), White (MIA), Caminiti (ATL), and Santucci (NYM) spent 96-100% of qualifying starts inside the 0.8-1.3 reference band. Different orgs, ages, and build-up paths — read the four together as case studies in what unbothered usage looked like in this cohort, not a target other arms should hit.

callouts.headline: Highest-IP pitchers who finished the season healthy: Hackenberg, Ford, Santucci.
callouts.body: Three pitchers who carried the most innings while staying in-band on ACWR and avoiding in-season injury flags: Hackenberg (ATL, 129 IP, 91% in-band, 3 levels), Ford (SEA, 125 IP, 100% in-band), Santucci (NYM, 122 IP, 96% in-band). Messick (CLE, 138 IP) also stayed healthy but carried two ACWR spikes and 1.88 max — high volume with a less-smooth ratio profile. Hess (NYY, 108 IP, 90% in-band) matched the metrics yet still ended up with a 27-day mid-season gap and injury history — clean visible workload data does not guarantee a clean season, because the CSV does not see bullpens or undisclosed issues.

callouts.headline: Age tracks pitch-count ceiling — and 20-21 is the noisiest middle.
callouts.body: Avg max P scales with age in this cohort: 18-19yo 82P, 20-21yo 88P, 22+ 94P. In-band ACWR % is not monotonic — 85% at 18-19, 76% at 20-21, 83% at 22+. Three of the five pitchers with an ACWR > 1.5 sit in the 20-21 group. With small per-group n we treat this as a descriptive observation about the cohort, not a generalization about all minor-league pitchers.

callouts.headline: Visible workload is necessary context, not sufficient evidence.
callouts.body: Knoth (offseason TJ), Meyer (2024 nagging issue), Nichols (three IL stints in 2024), Hess (27-day mid-season gap) — none of these stand out in pitch count or ACWR alone. Extended rest gaps (>15 days) coincide with reported injuries, but for most cases the gap IS the IL stint — that is confirmatory, not predictive. ACWR spikes also did not reliably precede injuries here: of 5 pitchers with a spike > 1.5, only Nichols had an injury pattern that overlapped the spike. Cijntje's three spikes all landed during his high-frequency piggyback phase where the 3-start chronic baseline sat in the 30-50P range — small absolute pitch-count increases register as large ratios when the denominator is that low, so the spikes are a metric artifact, not a workload overload signal. We surface workload visibility because that is what the CSV shows; bullpens, side work, mechanics, and perceived effort are not in scope here.

observed_patterns.title: Patterns observed in the cleanest seasons in this cohort
observed_patterns.intro: Of the pitchers who covered 95+ IP, six combined that volume with a high in-band ACWR rate (≥80%, max ≤1.4, no spikes > 1.5) and finished the season without an in-season injury flag: Hackenberg (ATL), Ford (SEA), Santucci (NYM), McLean (NYM), Baumann (ATL), White (MIA). The patterns below are what we observed in those six seasons. We are not claiming these patterns caused the outcome — only describing what the visible data showed.

items.title: Consistent weekly rhythm
items.body: All six held 6-7 day rest windows 85%+ of the time. Few unexplained compressions or extensions. Ford's 7-day rhythm is the strictest in the dataset (20 of 22 rest gaps at exactly 7 days). Rotation integrity is a shared feature of these seasons — we cannot tell from the CSV whether it was the org's deliberate plan or a happy byproduct of how the schedule fell.

items.title: Pitch-count caps expanded in step with promotions
items.body: Hackenberg opened at 70P and ended at 97P across three levels. Ford opened at 75P and ended at 89P. Santucci opened at 66P and ended at 81P. Caps climbed 15-25P over the season in staircase fashion, each step coinciding with a level move — not one aggressive jump, not stuck at a plateau. The CSV cannot tell us whether the cap rose because of the promotion or whether the promotion happened because the pitcher had earned the higher cap; the two move together.

items.title: All six earned at least one promotion in season
items.body: Hackenberg cleared three levels; the other five made at least one level jump. We can see that promotions happened and that pitch counts / ACWR stayed in their reference bands through the transitions; we cannot see why the org made each call from the CSV alone. What the data does show: none of the six had a regression or IL stint that pushed promotion timing back.

items.title: Max ACWR under 1.4 across all six seasons
items.body: Hackenberg 1.17, Ford 1.19, Santucci 1.29, McLean 1.28, Baumann 1.35, White 1.12. None exceeded 1.4 max ACWR. Contrast with Messick (CLE workhorse, 138 IP) at 1.88 max with 2 spikes — he stayed healthy too, but his ratio profile was different. The takeaway is descriptive: high-IP and clean ACWR can coexist in this cohort; high-IP without clean ACWR is also possible.

items.title: No unexplained mid-season gaps in these six seasons
items.body: Every gap above 10 days in this group was tied to a level promotion (8-11 days typical) or the All-Star break extension. No mystery 10+ day gaps. Across the broader cohort, when a gap of 15+ days appears without a promotion or ASB explanation, it almost always coincides with an injury event in the public reporting. So the absence of unexplained gaps is part of what makes these six seasons readable as 'clean.'

items.title: Five of six are 20+ years old
items.body: The lone 19yo is White (MIA) — who opened 2024 already capped near 75P thanks to 2023 buildup. Older pitchers in this sample carried more IP without ACWR distortion. The 18-19 group can also run high in-band % (85% on average) but does not carry enough total IP to enter this volume category, which is consistent with how orgs treat the youngest arms in the sample.

age_analysis.title: Age group patterns
age_analysis.intro: Across 24 pitchers, age tracks workload ceiling more closely than org or level does. The notable wrinkle: in-band ACWR % is not monotonic with age in this cohort — it dips at 20-21 and recovers at 22+. We are describing what the cohort showed, not generalizing to all minor-league pitchers.

groups.label: 18-19 years old
groups.takeaway: Tight caps (72-86P range). Low inning counts (avg 81 IP) — most pitchers top out around 70-100 innings. High ACWR discipline (85% sweet, zero spikes above 1.5 in the entire group). Clemmey and Doughty both capped hard at 80P. This is the 'protect the young arm' zone where orgs are most conservative and most uniform.

groups.label: 20-21 years old
groups.takeaway: The messy middle. Workload expands (avg 103 IP) and caps climb (88P avg), but ACWR discipline drops to 76%, max ACWR jumps to 1.54. Three of the five pitchers in the dataset with ACWR spikes above 1.5 sit here (Cijntje 2.08, Gill Hill 1.77, Nichols 1.66). This is the group where orgs test durability — sometimes with injury consequences (Nichols had three IL stints). Ford and Patick are the exceptions — both ran clean seasons inside their age-appropriate caps.

groups.label: 22+ years old
groups.takeaway: Workhorse zone. Avg 111 IP, 94P max — teams deploy them like legitimate starter prospects. ACWR discipline recovers to 83% as orgs have more confidence in the arm's durability. Three of the four highest-IP pitchers in the dataset sit here (Messick 138, Hackenberg 129, Santucci 122). But not everyone in this group is clean: Cunningham ran 94P max and ended up on shoulder IL; Hess ran 99P max and had a 27-day gap with prior injury history.

conclusions: Pitch count ceiling is primarily age-gated, not level-gated. Clemmey at 19 in Low-A capped at 80P; Messick at 23 in AA pushed to 97P. Same CLE organization (pre-trade for Clemmey), different ages, 17P cap difference.

ACWR discipline follows a U-shape with age, not a line. Tightest at 18-19 (85% sweet) and 22+ (83%), loosest at 20-21 (76%). The middle years are where orgs test the arm's ability to absorb spikes — sometimes intentionally, sometimes as a byproduct of promotion-era workload expansion.

Health-disruption cases correlate with pushing at or above age-appropriate ceiling. Cunningham (22yo) was hitting 92P+ regularly before his shoulder IL; Meyer (19yo) was at 79-85P right before the nagging issues began; Nichols (21yo) was at 82-87P across short-rest stretches before his first IL stint.

The healthiest high-volume seasons (Hackenberg, Ford, Santucci, McLean, Baumann, White) stayed INSIDE age-appropriate caps while accumulating total IP through frequency and rhythm integrity — not per-start overload.

Extended rest gaps (>15 days) coincide with reported injuries more reliably than ACWR spikes do — but remember these are largely confirmatory (the gap IS the IL stint), not predictive. 5 of 6 pitchers with gaps over 15 days had reported or inferred injury issues (Meccage, Meyer, Cunningham, Hess, Nichols); White's 20-day gap was the 2024 Futures Game plus rest. By contrast, only 1 of 5 pitchers with ACWR spikes above 1.5 had an injury pattern overlapping the spike (Nichols). Cijntje's three spikes were a metric artifact of a low-pitch-count piggyback phase (3-start chronic baseline at 30-50P makes ACWR mathematically volatile); Messick stayed healthy; Ziehl's came during a mid-season trade rebuild; Gill Hill had no formal injury.

org_rankings.title: Organization-level descriptive snapshot
org_rankings.intro: Mean share of starts in the 0.8–1.3 ACWR reference band across each org's sampled pitchers. Most orgs are 1–3 pitchers — directional only, not a 'team philosophy.' Pair every figure here with raw pitch counts before drawing inferences; ACWR alone can mislead.

orgs.note: Both Santucci and McLean ran clean high-volume seasons with AA promotions. Cleanest 2-pitcher sample.
Three pitchers, all clean. Hackenberg's 3-level year is the exemplar. Best 3-pitcher sample.
Clemmey (single-pitcher sample, directional) maintained 80P cap and clean ACWR across both orgs post-trade.
Dramatic intra-org split: White (100% sweet, zero issues) vs Meyer (nagging 2024 injury).
Tightest caps in the sample (75P ceiling for both Patick and Zazueta), but both are 19-20yo — could equally reflect age-appropriate scaling. Directional with n=2.
Age-calibrated protocol: Doughty (19) tight, Messick (23) workhorse. Messick's ACWR spikes drag the avg.

Both sampled pitchers (Cunningham, Hess) ran a 94-99P ceiling in their first pro season and both had in-season disruptions (shoulder IL, 27-day gap). With n=2 this is a pattern to track in future cohorts, not a proven org-wide approach.

Cijntje's ACWR-max figure is inflated by the low-pitch-count piggyback phase (3-4 day spacing, 15-87P range) — small absolute increases register as large ratios against a 30-50P chronic baseline. Ford's season is dataset-cleanest. Caps climb with both age and level in this 3-pitcher sample (Sloan 19→Ford 20→Cijntje 21); the two are confounded here.
Knoth's clean numbers preceded TJ — the alarming 'no warning' case. Meccage injury-disrupted.

Ziehl single-pitcher sample (directional only) — 3 ACWR spikes in the post-trade rebuild phase. Mid-season trade workload discontinuity; the 'aggressive' label reflects the trade disruption more than an org style.

Lowest sweet%. Across the 3 sampled 20-21yo pitchers: 87-92P cap range, Nichols injury-disrupted (3 IL stints), Gill Hill one ACWR spike, Harrison the only confirmed skipped-turn case in the dataset. With n=3 the 'aggressive' read is better-supported than most orgs here, but still a small sample — we'd want 5+ to call it a settled philosophy.

key_patterns.title: Short-start re-entry sits near pre-short workload, not below it
key_patterns.body: Median next-start-as-% of pre-short across the full dataset is 97% (mean 96%), across 55 qualifying short-start events. Most orgs re-enter within 5-15 points of the pre-short workload rather than dropping protection. Read the Short-starts tab for per-org detail — NYY's two 2025 draftees got the most tempered re-entries (median 52%), NYY/CHW's Ziehl post-trade the most aggressive (122%). Only one pitcher in the dataset has a confirmed skipped turn after a short start (Harrison, TB, around the All-Star break window).

key_patterns.title: Organization hard cap styles cluster clearly — within small samples
key_patterns.body: LAD sits tightest in the dataset at 75P max across both sampled pitchers (Patick, Zazueta), though with n=2 and both 19-20yo this could equally reflect age-appropriate scaling (directional). CLE/WAS 80P ceiling for Clemmey across both orgs (n=1 per org). CLE is age-calibrated in the sample we have: Doughty 80P at 19, Messick 97P at 23 (n=2). SEA caps climb with both age and level in its 3-pitcher sample (Sloan 72 at 19 Low-A → Ford 89 at 20 Low-A → Cijntje 99 at 21 AA) — age and level are confounded here and we can't separate them with n=3. MIL scales by level across its 3-pitcher sample (82-85 at Low-A, 92 at High-A). NYY's two 2024 college draftees (Cunningham, Hess) both ran 94-99P early in 2025 and both had in-season disruptions (shoulder IL, 27-day gap) — worth tracking forward, not strong enough at n=2 to call causal. ATL's 18-19 aggressive point comes from Caminiti alone at 93P (n=1 at that age in the org); TB's Harrison/Gill Hill 92P at 20-21 is n=2. Read these as starting hypotheses for future cohorts, not settled org philosophies.

key_patterns.title: Multi-level promotions happen cleanly when data supports it
key_patterns.body: Hackenberg (ATL 2024) went Rome → Mississippi → Gwinnett — three levels in one season, 129 IP, no injury. Messick (CLE 2024) and Hess (NYY 2025) both went High-A → AA. McLean (NYM 2024) and Santucci (NYM 2025) both climbed High-A → AA with clean performance. Each transition handled with an 8-11 day gap.

key_patterns.title: Invisible injury signatures — watch for these patterns
key_patterns.body: Four cases in this dataset show 'pushing through an undiagnosed issue' signatures that wouldn't trigger ACWR alarms. (1) Meyer (MIA 2024): two 19-21 day gaps without formal IL placement. His returns were short in IP (1-2 IP outings) but the pitch counts actually held within the 85-105% re-entry band of pre-gap starts (6/27 return: 88.8% of pre-gap pitches; 7/24 return: 88.7%) — so this is better read as 'limited innings on a tempered pitch count' rather than a broken workload protocol. The injury signature here is the IP-vs-pitch-count mismatch, not a pitch-count spike. (2) Nichols (TB 2024): three separate IL stints with low-volume outings in between — season capped at 71 IP. (3) Knoth (MIL 2024): completely clean workload pattern followed by offseason TJ — the scariest case, no visible signal. (4) Hess (NYY 2025): 27-day mid-season gap with injury history prior to the draft, ACWR looked fine throughout.

key_patterns.title: Aggressive first-year deployment — a pattern worth tracking, not yet a claim
key_patterns.body: Three first-pro-season cases in the dataset had both a high pitch-count ceiling and an in-season disruption: Cunningham (NYY 2025, 22yo college draftee) pushed to 92P+ in 5 of 7 pre-injury starts, then missed 65+ days with shoulder; Hess (NYY 2025, 22yo college draftee) ran 99P max in first pro season with a 27-day mid-season gap; Knoth (MIL 2024, 18yo prep) is the ambiguous case — chronic workload was building but nothing screamed warning, and his offseason TJ came after the CSV window. The sample is three pitchers (two of them from the same org) — too small to call the pattern causal. The honest framing: first-year deployment above 92P is worth flagging forward as a cohort to monitor, not presented as a settled org concern.

key_patterns.title: Extended rest gaps are confirmatory, not predictive — and ACWR spikes are noisy
key_patterns.body: Of 6 pitchers with a rest gap above 15 days, 5 had reported or inferred injury issues (Meccage, Meyer, Cunningham, Hess, Nichols). But read this as descriptive, not predictive — for 4 of those 5, the gap IS the IL stint. White's 20-day exception was the 2024 Futures Game plus a planned skip. The usable takeaway: when you see an unexplained gap of 15+ days in a pitcher's log, expect an injury in the context (even if no formal IL was reported). Meanwhile, of 5 pitchers with ACWR spikes above 1.5, only 1 had an injury pattern overlapping the spike (Nichols). Cijntje's three spikes are a metric artifact: they all landed during his high-frequency piggyback phase where the 3-start chronic baseline sat in the 30-50P range, and ACWR ratios go volatile when the denominator is low (a 15P → 45P jump reads as a 3.0 ACWR without meaning the workload is dangerous). Messick spiked twice as the CLE workhorse and stayed healthy; Ziehl's spike came in the post-trade rebuild; Gill Hill had one spike with no formal injury. ACWR spikes without context are noisy — and ACWR as a tool assumes a relatively stable chronic baseline, so it breaks down when a pitcher is deployed at highly variable per-outing volumes.

key_patterns.title: All-Star break rest splits into two patterns — outcomes vary, we can't attribute them
key_patterns.body: Minimal-disruption crowd (+2-3 days): Santucci, Meccage, Patick, Sloan, Birchard — shifted rotation and came back quickly. Extended-rest crowd (+4-5 days): Ford, Caminiti, Doughty, Harrison, White — got a full week off. Comeback-start outcomes were mixed in both groups (Ford came back 88P, Santucci came back 1.2 IP / 46P) but with two data points in each bucket we can't claim the break length drove the outcome — opponent quality, random variation, and other scheduling factors all overlap. The descriptive fact is the rest split; the causal story isn't in the CSV.
